How To Use • Help & Tips

Start with General Weights (Projection / Injury / Age/Exp / Team Context), then use Position Multipliers to match your league. Use Advanced Adjustments to change the team-context recipe and age curves. Hover or tap ⓘ for quick slider explanations.

⭐ Why Use This Big Board? ⭐

Most fantasy football big boards are ultimately based on human opinion. Even when analysts use data, the final ranks are often locked in — you get their assumptions whether you agree with them or not.

This big board is different by design. It starts with data, statistics, and historical trends — and then lets you decide how those inputs should be valued.

What makes it different
  • No baked-in rankings. There is no expert list hiding underneath.
  • No narrative bias. Players aren’t boosted because they’re hyped, popular, or “feel” exciting.
  • Every factor is measurable. Production, durability, age curves, and team environment are all quantified.
  • Everything is relative. Players are compared to their peers using consistent percentile-based scoring.

Data first — not opinion first

Rankings are built from real on-field outcomes: how much players score when they play, how often they miss time, where they are in their career arc, and the environments they play in.

Historical trends matter here. Age curves, breakout windows, decline phases, and team tendencies are applied consistently across the entire player pool — not adjusted on a player-by-player whim.

You control the philosophy

This site doesn’t assume there’s one “correct” way to rank players. Instead, it gives you the tools to express your draft philosophy.

  • Prefer safe, reliable players? Increase Injury Risk.
  • Want upside and youth? Increase Age & Experience.
  • Believe situation matters more than talent? Increase Team Context.
  • Care a lot about landing spots and free agency? Increase Offseason

Presets give you a quick starting point, and sliders let you fine-tune until the board matches how you actually draft.

Transparent and adjustable

Nothing here is a black box. When players move, it’s because a measurable factor changed or because you told the model to value something differently.

Bottom line: This board doesn’t tell you who to draft. It shows you how the rankings change when different data-driven ideas matter more — and lets you draft with confidence.

What Affects a Player’s Rank?

Every player starts with a score built from five main inputs: Projection, Injury Risk, Age/Exp, Team Context, and Offseason Impact.

Those pieces are combined into one total score, then adjusted by position multipliers, a small sample reliability adjustment, a major injury penalty, and finally a QB curve that pushes down non-elite QBs in 1QB-style rankings.

Big Picture (simplified formula)
Final Score ≈ (Projection × Projection Weight) + (Injury × Injury Weight) + (Age/Exp × Age/Exp Weight) + (Team Context × Team Context Weight) + (Offseason × Offseason Weight)

Then Apply: Position Multiplier × Sample Reliability × Major Injury Penalty
Then Apply: QB Curve for lower-end QBs in 1QB-style rankings.

1) Projection (Expected Fantasy Output)

Projection is the production backbone of the model. It is based on a player’s fantasy points per game history and uses a 3-year weighted blend.

  • 2025 weight: 0.75
  • 2024 weight: 0.20
  • 2023 weight: 0.05

That means last season matters most, but older seasons still provide a little stability. Missing older seasons do not automatically punish younger players.

What the slider does: Increasing Projection makes the board more production-driven. Lowering it allows the other factors to matter more in close calls.

2) Injury Risk (Durability History)

Injury Risk is based on how often a player has actually been available across the seasons that exist in the data. It is not a future medical prediction — it is a durability history score.

  • Players who regularly miss games grade worse.
  • Players who consistently stay on the field grade better.
  • Players are only judged on seasons with actual games data available.

What the slider does: Increasing Injury Risk boosts safer profiles and knocks down players with shakier availability histories.

3) Age & Experience (Career Stage)

Age/Exp is your career-stage score. It uses a position-specific historical curve, because RBs, WRs, TEs, and QBs do not age the same way.

The model uses a triangle-style age/experience curve: players near the historical sweet spot for their position score best, while players farther away from that sweet spot score worse.
  • RBs peak earlier and fall off faster.
  • WRs often benefit from the Year 2 / Year 3 growth window and hold value longer.
  • TEs usually develop later than WRs and RBs.
  • QBs are the most stable and usually age best.

In Advanced, you can move those position curves yourself with sliders like RB Prime Age, RB Falloff Age, WR Prime Age, and so on.

What the slider does: Increasing Age/Exp makes those historical career-stage trends matter more.

4) Team Context (Environment)

Team Context measures how fantasy-friendly a player’s team environment is. This is not about player talent — it is about situation.

  • Better offenses create better fantasy environments.
  • Better offensive lines especially help RBs and overall efficiency.
  • Pass-heavy teams help WRs and TEs.
  • QB quality matters a lot for pass catchers.
  • Team pace affects how many plays an offense can run.
  • New coaching changes create a small scheme-related penalty.

Team Context is position-specific. A rushing QB does not affect RBs the same way it affects QBs. An RB-by-committee flag matters for RBs, not for WRs.

In Advanced, you can change the exact recipe for each position by adjusting how much things like Offense, O-Line, QB Play, Pass Rate, Run Rate, Pace, Rushing QB, RB Committee, and Scheme Change matter.

5) Offseason Impact (Landing Spot + Opportunity + Competition)

Offseason Impact is the model’s way of reacting to player movement and role changes. It combines three pieces:

  • Landing Spot Delta: did the moved player end up in a better or worse football environment?
  • Vacated Opportunity Delta: did teammates benefit because usage left the old team?
  • Incoming Competition Delta: did teammates lose value because a new player arrived?

The logic is also position-aware. WR and TE movement mostly affects WR/TE teammates, while RB movement mostly affects other RBs.

What the slider does: Increasing Offseason makes new landing spots, vacated targets, and added competition matter more in the rankings.

6) Sample Reliability

The board applies a small sample reliability adjustment based on total games played in the available data. This is meant to slightly reduce trust in profiles built on very small samples.

In practice, this adjustment is usually subtle — it is more of a stabilizer than a major ranking driver.

7) Major Injury Penalty

On top of normal injury history, the board applies an additional major injury penalty when a player has a tagged severe recent injury.

  • The penalty scales by severity.
  • It is capped so one injury tag cannot completely destroy a player’s score by itself.

8) QB Curve

After the main score is calculated, QBs get one more adjustment: the board keeps the very top QBs strong, then increasingly pushes down the lower-end QBs.

This is intentional. In most 1QB leagues, the difference between QB8 and QB16 usually matters much less than the same range at RB or WR.

That is why the board naturally behaves more like a normal 1QB draft board unless you raise the QB multiplier.

9) What is shown in the player details?

The player detail panel also shows helpful diagnostic stats that are not directly their own core weight:

  • Target Share and Target Competition
  • Season ranks by year
  • Player Snapshot summary text
  • Strength / Concern chips
  • Team context inputs and Offseason Outlook

These help explain why a player looks the way they do, even if those exact display labels are not each individual weight sliders.

How The Sliders Work

All sliders in this UI are offset sliders.

  • 0.00 means “use the built-in default.”
  • Positive offsets increase the default value.
  • Negative offsets decrease the default value.

In other words, the sliders usually do not replace the model defaults — they nudge them up or down.

Why this matters:

  • Small changes can still move a lot of players because ranks are relative.
  • Multiple small changes can stack together.
  • If you want the original board back, hit Reset.
General Weights [How To Use]
  • Projection: Higher = production history matters more. Lower = the board becomes less purely stats-first.
  • Injury Risk: Higher = durability matters more. Lower = you are more willing to bet on risky health profiles.
  • Age/Exp: Higher = historical career-stage trends matter more. Lower = age curves matter less.
  • Team Context: Higher = situation matters more across all positions. Lower = team environment becomes a smaller nudge.
  • Offseason: Higher = new landing spots, vacated opportunity, and added competition matter more. Lower = offseason movement has less influence.

Good rule of thumb: start by moving just one general weight at a time. If the board moves more than expected, reduce that last adjustment slightly.

Position Multipliers [How To Use]

Position multipliers change the value of entire positions, not individual players. This is the fastest way to make the board fit your league format.

  • 1QB leagues: keep QB near baseline or even a little lower.
  • Superflex / 2QB: raise QB noticeably.
  • Heavy flex formats: RB and WR often deserve more weight.
  • TE premium: raise TE.

Important: changing a position multiplier can reshuffle a large chunk of the board very quickly. That is normal, because you are changing the value of an entire position pool at once.

Presets [How To Use]

Presets are just starter philosophies. They apply a bundle of slider changes at once, and then you can keep fine-tuning from there.

  • Default: baseline board with no slider offsets.
  • High Floor: leans safer with more durability and team stability.
  • High Ceiling: leans more aggressive and upside-focused.
  • Talent Over Team: reduces the importance of situation.
  • Team Over Talent: leans harder into environment and strong team signals.
  • Zero RB: lowers RB value as a group and nudges WR/TE builds.

Presets are not permanent modes. They just set the sliders for you.

Advanced [How To Use]

Advanced lets you change the model itself in two important ways:

  • Age/Exp Curves: move where each position tends to peak or decline.
  • Team Context Recipe: change which team signals matter more for each position.

Use Advanced when:

  • You think RBs should age better or worse than the default model assumes.
  • You think WRs should be more tied to QB play.
  • You want O-line to matter more for RBs.
  • You want pass rate, pace, or scheme changes to matter more or less by position.

Best practice: make small changes here. Advanced sliders directly change the recipe behind the score, so they can have bigger downstream effects than the main weights.

Examples

Example 1: You increase Team Context

  • Players on stronger offenses, faster teams, and better environments tend to rise.
  • Players on weaker or slower offenses tend to fall.

Example 2: You increase WR → QB Play in Advanced

  • WRs tied to strong QB situations gain even more.
  • WRs in shaky passing environments lose ground.

Example 3: You increase Offseason

  • Players with stronger landing spots or more vacated opportunity rise.
  • Players facing new competition tend to fall.

Key idea: General sliders control how much something matters. Advanced sliders control what that thing means.

Troubleshooting / FAQ
  • Why did one slider move so many players? Rankings are relative. If one group rises, another group has to fall.
  • Why does the board change even when I only made a tiny move? Small weight shifts can stack across several inputs at once.
  • Why don’t young players get crushed for missing old seasons? The projection and injury formulas ignore missing seasons instead of treating them as zeroes.
  • Why are lower-end QBs pushed down? The model has a built-in QB curve for more realistic 1QB-style rankings.
  • Why does Team Context feel different by position? Because the recipe is position-specific. RBs, WRs, TEs, and QBs do not use the same team signals.
  • What does Reset do? It clears all offsets back to 0.00 and returns the board to its default model settings.
